aliases: Thick Toast with Meat Floss and Condensed Milk

english: Thick Toast with Meat Floss and Condensed Milk

food_subgroup: Toasted bread with savoury and sweet toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a generic 'toast (food)' class. No specific class exists for floss thick toast; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and bakery products broadly. No specific code for floss-topped thick toast exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for floss thick toast as a specific prepared food item.

serving.description: 1 serving (2 slices thick toast with floss and condensed milk)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving consists of two slices of thick white toast topped with meat floss and a drizzle of condensed milk, weighing approximately 120 g.

nutrient_source.title: Kaya Toast / Thick Toast with Toppings — HPB Singapore Foodo and USDA FoodData Central component estimation
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'Floss Thick Toast' was found. Nutrient values are estimated by component assembly: thick white toast (HPB Foodo: Bread, white, toasted), meat floss (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Pork, cured, dried, cooked — used as proxy for meat floss), and condensed milk (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy: Milk, condensed, sweetened). Proportions estimated as approximately 65 g thick toast, 20 g meat floss, 15 g condensed milk per 100 g composite. Values represent a blended per-100 g estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — White bread GI reference
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Floss Thick Toast. GI estimated at 70 based on white toast as the dominant carbohydrate vehicle. University of Sydney GI Database lists white bread/toast at GI 70–75 (glucose reference). Thick white toast is used as the primary GI anchor. Meat floss and condensed milk contribute protein, fat, and sugar but do not substantially lower the overall GI given the high refined-starch base. GL per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 58.2) / 100 ≈ 40.7, rounded to 40.9 accounting for condensed milk sugar contribution.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from meat floss and bread
hypertension.guidance: Floss thick toast contains approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g, primarily from the meat floss and bread; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index refined white bread base with added condensed milk sugar
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 70 and a glycemic load of approximately 41 per serving, floss thick toast poses a significant postprandial glucose challenge for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes and should be consumed only occasionally in small portions, ideally paired with a protein or fibre source to blunt the glycemic response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from meat floss and condensed milk, combined with refined carbohydrate load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Floss thick toast provides approximately 2.9 g saturated fat and 48.5 g refined carbohydrate per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption and consider lower-saturated-fat alternatives to reduce atherogenic risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Floss Thick Toast is a popular Singaporean kopitiam-style breakfast and snack item consisting of thick-cut white bread that is toasted until golden and crisp, then generously topped with meat floss (also known as rousong or serunding — a dry, fluffy, savoury condiment made from slow-cooked shredded pork or chicken) and finished with a drizzle of sweetened condensed milk. The dish is a signature offering at Toast Box, a well-known Singaporean café chain, and is widely available across hawker centres and coffee shops in Singapore. The flavour profile combines the savoury, umami-rich meat floss with the sweet creaminess of condensed milk against the crisp, slightly buttery toast base, creating a contrast of textures and a sweet-savoury taste balance characteristic of Singaporean breakfast culture. Nutritionally, floss thick toast is energy-dense, providing approximately 318 kcal per 100 g, with a moderate protein contribution from the meat floss (approximately 12.5 g per 100 g), moderate fat (approximately 7.8 g per 100 g), and a high refined carbohydrate load (approximately 48.5 g per 100 g). The glycemic index is high (approximately 70), making this food a significant postprandial glucose challenge. Sodium content is moderate to high at approximately 420 mg per 100 g due to the seasoned meat floss. Clinically, this food is of concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high GI and glycemic load, and for those with hypertension due to its sodium content. It is best consumed as an occasional treat rather than a daily staple.
common_user_queries: How many calories in floss thick toast?
Is floss toast suitable for diabetics?
Thick toast with condensed milk and floss nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry for Floss Thick Toast was found. Nutrient values are estimated by component assembly using HPB Foodo (white toast) and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (meat floss proxy, condensed milk). GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white toast as the primary analogous food. Confidence is medium because the component-assembly method introduces uncertainty in exact proportions used by individual restaurants.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are component-estimated: approximately 65 g thick white toast, 20 g meat floss, 15 g condensed milk per 100 g composite. Actual restaurant proportions may vary. 2. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used (HPB Foodo white toast and USDA SR Legacy meat floss/condensed milk entries do not provide vitamin D values). 3. Vitamin E is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 4. Selenium is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate based on white toast (University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for floss thick toast exists in the literature. 6. Halal status is listed as false because pork floss is commonly used; chicken floss variants may be halal-certified depending on the restaurant. Toast Box Singapore uses chicken floss and is generally considered halal-friendly, but this record defaults to the general case. 7. Vitamin C is 0.0 — confirmed zero as white toast, meat floss, and condensed milk contain negligible or no vitamin C; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component sources.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed full multilingual local_names for Singaporean cuisine category (Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi).
Estimated per_100g nutrients via component assembly from HPB Foodo and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy due to absence of exact HPB Foodo entry.
Assigned GI of 70 using University of Sydney GI Database white toast analogous estimate; documented estimation basis.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit source-gap reasons in notes.
Documented vitamin_c_mg = 0.0 as confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component sources.
Verified aliases_by_language and flat aliases consistency — all language-specific alias strings present verbatim in flat aliases array.
Set ontology match types to closest_parent for FoodOn and FoodEx2; none for SNOMED CT — all within allowed values.
Included Type 2 Diabetes with human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' and machine key type2_diabetes.
